title: Designing a grow facility in 3D before you build it
sub: How to lay out rooms, equipment and airflow in a 3D model on your screen, so you catch expensive mistakes before anyone pours concrete.

ref_lookup.threejs-repo: mrdoob and contributors. three.js — JavaScript 3D Library [WebGL/WebGPU scene-graph rendering library]. GitHub repository (MIT License). Accessed 2026-06-22.

Spatial examination of leaf-boundary-layer conductance using artificial leaves for assessment of light airflow within a plant canopy under different controlled greenhouse conditions. Agricultural and Forest Meteorology, 280, 107773. DOI:10.1016/j.agrformet.2019.107773
ref_lookup.ibc-2024-1011-5-2-stairs: International Code Council (2024). 2024 International Building Code (IBC), Section 1011.5.2 — Riser height and tread depth (stair riser 7 in. max / 4 in. min; rectangular tread 11 in. min).
ref_lookup.wac-314-55-083-cannabis-security: Washington State Liquor and Cannabis Board. WAC 314-55-083 — Security and traceability requirements for cannabis licensees (surveillance of all entrances/exits, processing/storage/destruction areas and POS; min. 640x470 resolution; min. 10 fps; recordings retained >=45 days; storage device secured against tampering/theft).

sections.title: What 3D facility planning is (and why it beats a paper plan)
sections.summary: A grow facility is a building full of rooms, equipment, airflow paths and security cameras, and a flat 2D floor plan only experts can fully read. A 3D model is the same plan rebuilt on screen as a thing you can rotate, zoom into and click — instantly legible to investors, electricians, inspectors and staff. The big idea in the source is that the floor plan becomes a data file (a list of rooms and dimensions), and code draws the building from that data, so re-planning every harvest is just editing numbers. The reference example is a real two-storey licensed cultivation facility measuring 14.8 by 16.7 metres.
points: A 3D model makes spatial relationships visible: airflow paths, camera sightlines, bench density, duct and drain runs
Built with Three.js, a free web library, so the whole thing is one HTML file that opens in any browser with no install
"The floor plan is a data file" means changing the building is editing JSON, not redrawing — ideal for option studies like 'what if we fit a fourth bench?'
Same model serves four jobs at once: design tool, compliance exhibit for licensing, staff training aid, and live monitoring dashboard

figures.name: paper_vs_3d
figures.desc: Side-by-side: a dense flat 2D floor plan on the left, the same plan as a rotatable labelled 3D building on the right, with arrows showing who reads each (architect vs everyone else).

claims_needing_cite: Three.js is the de-facto standard WebGL scene-graph library (operational/tooling claim from source)

sections.title: Key terms, defined from zero
sections.summary: Before anything else, here is the vocabulary. None of these require prior knowledge — they are just the words this topic keeps using. Read this once and the rest of the paper reads easily.
points: 3D model — a picture of the building you can rotate, zoom and click on a screen, not a fixed photo
Three.js — a free code library that draws 3D scenes inside a normal web browser
Floor plan — the architect's flat top-down drawing of walls, doors and rooms
Render / scene — 'render' means draw the picture; the 'scene' is everything in the model (walls, benches, lights)
JSON / data file — a plain list of facts (room names, sizes, positions) the code reads to build the model
FOV cone — 'field of view' cone; a translucent wedge showing exactly what a camera can see
Fit-out — the equipment and furniture added to a bare room (benches, lights, dehumidifiers)
Digital twin — a 3D model wired to live sensors so it shows real current temperature and humidity

figures.name: term_map
figures.desc: Two-column glossary table: term on the left, one-line plain definition on the right, covering all eight terms above.

sections.title: Model the building as data, not as a drawing
sections.summary: The single most important decision in the source: do not draw the building by hand in code — describe it as data and let code interpret it. The reference schema needs only four record types: rooms, wall runs with openings, equipment, and devices. Each room is stored as a rectangle [x, y, width, depth] in metres, walls are centre-lines with openings positioned by distance along the run, and a global wall thickness (0.15 m) avoids a whole class of typos. Crucially, all dimensions use one unit equals one metre, so the millimetre numbers on the architect's plan (4800, 9200) get divided by 1000 once at data-entry time.
points: Four record types cover almost any grow building: rooms, walls, equipment, devices
A room is just [x, y, width, depth] of its interior footprint, in metres
Doors, the 4.6 m roller door, and pass-throughs are all the same thing: an 'opening' on a wall with a width and a head (top) height
One model file can drive a 2D plan, a bench-count report and the 3D scene from the same numbers — one source of truth
Because it is data, the model survives every re-plan; you edit numbers, not geometry

figures.name: schema_shape
figures.desc: Annotated JSON snippet showing the four record types (rooms, walls, equipment, devices) with callouts explaining rect = [x,y,w,d], opening 'at' distance, and device 'aim' bearing.

figures.name: data_to_views
figures.desc: Flow: single JSON data file branches to three outputs — 2D SVG plan, bench-count report, 3D scene — illustrating one source of truth.

sections.title: Building the shell: floors, walls, openings and stairs
sections.summary: With the data in place, code builds the physical shell. Floor slabs are 2D shapes 'extruded' (pushed up) into thickness, and they can include holes — which you need for the stairwell void. Each wall run is split by its openings into solid segments with a lintel filling the gap above each door. The reference building's entire envelope and partitions are about 25 data rows fed to one wall function. Stairs are a simple loop of step boxes: this facility climbs 3.25 m over a 4.5 m run as 16 steps of 203 mm each, which also doubles as a sanity check that the stair actually fits its allotted space.
points: Floor slabs are extruded shapes that can carry holes for stairwells and service voids
A wall is a centre-line plus openings; openings split it into segments with lintels above — no complex geometry needed
Each storey gets its own group, so switching floors is just hiding one and showing the other (labels hide automatically too)
Stairs as a step loop double as a buildability check: 16 risers at 203 mm over 4.5 m proves the stair fits before construction
Corrugated roller-door texture and similar details come from 10 lines of code, no image files

figures.name: wall_run
figures.desc: One wall run drawn as boxes: solid segment, an opening (door, head 2.05 m), lintel above it, another segment — labelling 'at' as distance along the run.

figures.name: stair_check
figures.desc: Stair sanity-check table: total rise 3.25 m, run 4.5 m, 16 risers at 203 mm, treads at 281 mm, with a pass/fail note on fit.

claims_needing_cite: Standard stair riser/tread proportions for buildability (cite a building-code or stair-design source — scientific/regulatory)

sections.title: Fit-out: benches, plants, climate gear and airflow
sections.summary: Fit-out is what turns a generic building into a grow facility, and every piece is built from simple shapes — no modelling software. The reference rooms use 1.2 by 7.6 m rolling benches, three per flower room, giving 27.4 m² of canopy per room (about 62% of floor area, a number the model shows at a glance). Plants are the highest-count object (210 plant sites here) so they are 'instanced' — drawn in one batch — which is the single biggest performance lever. Climate gear (dehumidifiers, carbon filter/fan units hung at 2.45 m, mini-split AC heads, CO2 cylinders) each get a tiny builder, and the plan's 3500 m³/h horizontal airflow is rendered as toggleable arrows that contractors read instantly.
points: Benches, lights and racks are generated from numbers, so the model surfaces canopy density (62% of floor) automatically
210 plants are drawn as one batch ('instancing') — the biggest single speed win
Each equipment type (dehumidifier, filter/fan, AC head, CO2 tank) is a small reusable builder sized from datasheets
Airflow shown as arrow glyphs makes the 3500 m³/h horizontal airflow visible to HVAC contractors and inspectors
Placing equipment in 3D reveals clashes early: a filter hung at 2.45 m over an aisle, AC heads above doors, dehumidifiers sitting on benches

figures.name: equipment_recipes
figures.desc: Equipment schedule: item, primitive-shape recipe, and reference-facility count (e.g. dehumidifiers 7, carbon filter/fan 10, AC heads 8, CO2 cylinders 6).

figures.name: canopy_density
figures.desc: Bar chart of canopy area vs total floor area per flower room (27.4 m² canopy of 44 m² floor = 62%).

figures.name: airflow_loop
figures.desc: Top-down zone view of one flower room showing the racetrack airflow arrows over the three benches.

claims_needing_cite: Target horizontal airflow rate of 3500 m³/h for a flower room (cite a horticulture/CEA airflow guideline — scientific)
Canopy-to-floor ratio as a design benchmark (operational guidance unless tied to a yield study)

sections.title: The security and compliance layer you can actually see
sections.summary: Licensed cultivation is audited on its security plan, and modelling that layer turns a checklist into something you can walk an inspector through. Each camera gets a translucent FOV cone whose length is its usable range and whose width is its lens angle, so blind spots show up as un-tinted floor. The reference facility models roughly 22 cameras (20 bullet, 1 doorbell, 1 PTZ), 16 sirens, 2 floor-bolted drug safes, a walk-in vault (the drying room itself), and PoE/UPS racks (three 6U plus one 13U). Grouping every device under one toggle lets you flip between 'facility tour' and 'security audit' views.
points: Camera FOV cones answer licensing questions visually: do three flower-room cameras cover all three benches? Does the hallway pair leave a lobby gap?
Blind spots appear as floor with no cone tint — far easier to spot than reading a coverage list
Sirens, drug safes, the walk-in vault and network/power racks are all modelled, because cable runs and UPS placement are part of the security story
One checkbox shows or hides the whole security layer for audit mode vs tour mode
The roof PTZ camera's 70-degree, 12 m cone is checked against the upper open-plan office for intrusion coverage

figures.name: fov_coverage
figures.desc: Top-down flower room with three camera cones overlaid; any floor area outside all cones highlighted as a candidate blind spot.

figures.name: device_schedule
figures.desc: Device schedule: cameras 22 (20 bullet/1 doorbell/1 PTZ), sirens 16, drug safes 2, vault 1, PoE+UPS racks 3x6U + 1x13U, with placement logic.

claims_needing_cite: Specific security requirements (camera coverage, safes, vault) are jurisdiction-dependent licensing rules (cite the relevant cultivation licence/regulation — regulatory)

sections.title: A step-by-step path from plan to clickable model
sections.summary: Here is the practical order of work the source implies, with nothing skipped. You start by reading dimension chains off the architect's plan, transcribe them into the data file, then let the builders draw shell, fit-out and security, and finally add navigation and click-to-inspect. The whole stack is one HTML file, a JSON block and roughly 600 lines of generator code — and you can lift the source straight from the reference document.
points: 1. Read the dimension chains (e.g. 4800 + 4632.40 + 4800 across the top) and divide mm by 1000 to get metres
2. Transcribe rooms, walls, equipment and devices into the JSON schema — this is the real work; it becomes your source of truth
3. Run the shell builders (slabs, walls, stairs), then fit-out builders (benches, plants, climate), then the security layer
4. Add navigation: an orbit camera plus a one-click top view that reproduces the original 2D plan
5. Add HTML labels and click-to-inspect so each room and device reports its own contents — a self-documenting facility register
6. To reuse: save the page, copy the importmap and script block, replace the data tables with your own plan

figures.name: workflow_steps
figures.desc: Six-step flow from 'read dimension chains' through 'transcribe to JSON', 'build shell', 'fit-out', 'security layer', to 'add interaction'.

sections.title: Common pitfalls and how to dodge them
sections.summary: Most mistakes come from a handful of repeatable errors the source calls out. The biggest is modelling in code instead of data, which makes every re-plan painful. On the rendering side, adding a real light per grow fixture (14 of them) destroys the frame rate — use one sun plus glowing surfaces instead. Pure-white walls blow out under the realistic tone mapping, so use a slightly warm off-white. And when picking objects with the mouse, raycast against a curated list, never the whole scene, so users cannot accidentally select walls.
points: Modelling in code, not data — the cardinal sin; you lose your single source of truth and every re-plan hurts
One real light per fixture: 14 shadow-casting lights kill performance; use one directional 'sun' plus emissive (glowing) surfaces
Pure white walls blow out under ACES tone mapping — use a warm off-white (0xe8e6e0) at high roughness
Default shadow camera too big makes shadows mushy — size it to the building
Picking against the whole scene lets users select walls — raycast a curated 'pickables' list instead
Forgetting wall thickness as a global (0.15 m) reintroduces a class of typos

figures.name: pitfall_fix
figures.desc: Two-column table: common pitfall on the left, the source's recommended fix on the right, for the six items above.

sections.title: What 3D planning realistically gets you — and what it does not
sections.summary: Set expectations honestly. This kind of model is light and fast: the reference demo draws a full two-storey facility with about 450 meshes plus instanced plants at 60 frames per second on integrated graphics, and scales to campuses by instancing and merging geometry while keeping draw calls under about 300. Primitives carry you surprisingly far, but when you need a photoreal hero asset you author it in Blender and import it via glTF. The model is a planning, compliance and monitoring aid — it is not a structural-engineering or code-compliance sign-off, and the security and airflow targets it visualises still need a qualified professional to validate.
points: Performance is generous: ~450 meshes plus instanced plants at 60 fps on integrated graphics; keep draw calls under ~300 for low-end devices
It scales to multi-building campuses via instancing, geometry merging and lazy-loading storeys
Simple shapes go far; reach for Blender + glTF only for a detailed 'hero' asset
Wire it to live sensors (MQTT/Home Assistant) and it becomes a monitoring dashboard — the floor plan turns into the UI
It is a design, compliance-exhibit and training aid, not an engineering sign-off; targets it shows still need professional validation

figures.name: perf_budget
figures.desc: Performance budget table: meshes ~450, target 60 fps, draw-call ceiling ~300, pixel-ratio cap 2, one shadow-casting light.

figures.name: capability_ladder
figures.desc: Ladder from primitives-only model, to glTF hero assets, to React dashboard, to live-data digital twin — showing where each becomes worth the effort.

claims_needing_cite: 60 fps with ~450 meshes on integrated graphics (operational performance figure from source — reproduce-to-verify rather than cite)
